ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific packaged RTD latte subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers coffee and coffee substitutes as a category; no specific RTD packaged latte code exists. Category-level match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged ready-to-drink latte. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one 240 ml can or bottle of packaged latte, equivalent to about 240 g.

nutrient_source.title: Latte (packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged latte (CR ID D09010044). All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged RTD latte in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: sweetened milk-based coffee beverage with sugar. Milk has a GI of approximately 31–34 (low); added sugar (sucrose) has a GI of approximately 65; the blended beverage with 6.84g sugar/100g and milk protein moderating glycaemic response yields an estimated GI of ~55. Glycemic load per 240g serving calculated as: (55 × 20.14g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 11.1. GI of 55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database criteria (GI ≤ 55 is low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.84g sugar/100g) exceeding the 5g/100g SSB threshold; insulin-driven sodium retention risk per AHA 2018 guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged latte due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification (6.84g sugar/100g), which independently raises blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention regardless of its relatively low sodium content (44mg/100g); choose unsweetened black coffee or zero-sugar latte variants instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification (6.84g sugar/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.09g/100g); rapid glycaemic impact from liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: This packaged latte is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 6.84g sugar/100g and only 0.09g dietary fibre/100g, triggering the ADA diabetes hard gate; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid it entirely and opt for unsweetened black coffee or plain milk without added sugar, as liquid sugar from SSBs is absorbed fastest and causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk; potential palm-based creamer saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of this sweetened packaged latte contributes to cardiovascular disease risk through its SSB sugar load (6.84g/100g), which is associated with dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and endothelial dysfunction per AHA dietary guidelines; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate SSB intake and check product labels for palm-based creamers that add saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.84g/100g) negates potential anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of coffee; processed formulation with possible pro-inflammatory palm-based creamer
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Although coffee contains chlorogenic acid polyphenols with potential anti-inflammatory properties, this packaged latte's added sugar content (6.84g/100g) and processed formulation negate any anti-inflammatory benefit; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened black coffee or kopi o kosong to obtain polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged latte (ready-to-drink) is a commercially produced, ultra-processed coffee beverage combining coffee extract, milk, sugar, and often a creamer base, sold in cans or bottles for immediate consumption. It delivers a mild coffee flavour with a sweet, creamy taste and smooth liquid texture. Per 100g it provides 49 kcal, 1.37g protein, 1.13g fat, 8.39g carbohydrate, and 6.84g sugar, with low dietary fibre (0.09g) and moderate sodium (44mg). The high sugar-to-fibre ratio classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA and AHA clinical guidelines, making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 11.1 per standard 240ml serving. Packaged lattes are widely consumed in Singapore and across Asia as a convenient breakfast or on-the-go beverage. Clinically, the primary concern is the SSB classification: liquid sugar from sweetened beverages is absorbed faster than solid food sugar, producing rapid postprandial glucose spikes and independently raising blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention. Consumers seeking the flavour of latte without metabolic risk should choose unsweetened variants or plain black coffee. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09010044, confidence 0.95).

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09010044 at confidence 0.95. All primary macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from an approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence for GI component only) as no direct RTD latte GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per HPB source D09010044; this is a confirmed zero per the approved HPB Singapore Foodo database entry — iron is present at below-detection or negligible levels in this milk-coffee formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this packaged latte formulation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09010044 as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk-coffee beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct RTD packaged latte GI measurement was found. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextual descriptors only; this food is classified as an SSB and is not recommended for diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease management despite its low absolute calorie and sodium values per 100g. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database criteria (GI <= 55 is low).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added missing veritas_qc top-level section which caused g1 hard gate failure in iteration 2. All other sections preserved from iteration 2 draft. Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 12.5 to 11.1 for arithmetic consistency with GL = (55 × 20.14) / 100 = 11.1.
